---
title: Customizing
description: Per-theme settings, custom templates, exporting and importing theme data.
---

Once you've activated a theme and arranged its templates in the [theme builder](/docs/themes/theme-builder), the remaining customization surface is **per-theme settings** (colors, fonts, logo, layout overrides) and **custom templates** (theme variants for specific pages).

## Per-theme settings

Every theme owns its own settings namespace. Open **Themes → \<theme\> → Settings** to see what the active theme exposes.

A typical theme exposes:

| Setting | What it controls |
| --- | --- |
| **Header logo** | The image (or SVG) rendered in the header. Picked from the media library. Falls back to the site title as text when unset. |
| **Logo height** | Height of the header logo in pixels. The width auto-scales. |
| **Favicon** | The browser-tab icon. Stored as a data URL on the theme so it survives storage-backend swaps. |
| **Color tokens** | The theme's brand palette. Usually a handful of named colors (primary, secondary, accent, surface) exposed to templates as CSS custom properties. |
| **Display font** | The font used for headings, titles, and other display surfaces. Picked from a curated list the theme ships with. |
| **Body font** | The font used for body prose. |
| **Layout defaults** | Theme-specific knobs like "sidebar width", "max content width", "show breadcrumbs by default". |

Each theme decides which fields to expose. The NextPresso default theme has roughly a dozen settings; richer themes can have many more.

Changes save on every interaction — no Save button.

## Custom templates

Beyond the eight built-in template surfaces (Homepage, Single Page, Single Post, Pillar Post, Topic Archive, Search Results, Author Profile, 404), you can create **custom templates** for specific pages or post types.

A custom template is a variant of a built-in template. Example use cases:

- A **Landing page** variant of Single Page for marketing pages (no header / no footer / full-bleed).
- A **Long-form** variant of Single Post for tutorial pages (table-of-contents sidebar, wider content column).
- An **Author Profile** variant for a specific contributor with a custom intro section.

### Creating a custom template

1. **Themes → \<theme\> → Open builder**.
2. In the template tabs, click **+ New template**.
3. Pick the **parent template** (the built-in template the variant is based on).
4. Name it (e.g. "Landing").
5. The builder opens with the parent template's content as the starting point. Edit freely.

### Assigning a custom template

Once the template exists:

1. Edit a page or post.
2. In the right rail, find the **Template** field.
3. Pick the custom template from the dropdown.

The page now renders with the custom template instead of the parent.

### Custom templates and the public renderer

When the public renderer needs to render `/<page-slug>` and the page has `template: "landing"`:

1. Look up the `landing` theme_data row.
2. If found, render with its puckData.
3. If not found (e.g. the template was deleted), fall back to the parent template.

The fallback means deleting a custom template won't break pages assigned to it — they revert to the parent's rendering.

## Exporting and importing theme data

For sites with significant customization, it's useful to develop in one environment and deploy to another. The theme data export/import flow lets you move customizations between sites.

### Exporting

**Themes → \<theme\> → "•••" → Export theme data**.

You get a JSON file containing every theme_data row for that theme: shared parts (header, footer, sidebars), templates (Homepage, Single Page, etc.), and custom templates. The file does NOT include site content (posts, pages, media) — only the theme arrangement.

### Importing

**Themes → \<theme\> → "•••" → Import theme data → pick file**.

NextPress validates the file (theme slug matches, schema version compatible) and shows a confirmation diff: which templates and parts would be replaced. After confirming, the import overwrites the current theme data wholesale.

Step-up auth required (it's a destructive operation if you've customized the destination).

### Use case: dev → prod

1. In dev, finish your theme customizations.
2. Export the theme data.
3. Activate the theme in prod (clones the theme's defaults).
4. Import the dev export. Your dev customizations are now in prod.

### Use case: backup before a big change

Always export before doing destructive editing or running `Reset all`. The export is a small JSON file — version it in a private gist or git repo for instant rollback.

## Theme-specific CSS

Themes can ship their own CSS for in-template styling that goes beyond what widget settings cover. By convention, theme CSS lives at `themes/<slug>/styles/theme.css` and is imported by the theme's template files.

The built-in NextPresso theme exposes a small set of `.np-*` utility classes its widgets use; custom themes are free to define whatever they need. Theme CSS isn't editable via the admin — it ships in the theme's source files.

## Color tokens

Most themes expose their color palette as CSS custom properties at the `:root` level, named something like:

```
--theme-primary: #2A3A5B;
--theme-accent:  #2B944F;
--theme-surface: #ffffff;
```

The values are read from the per-theme settings, so changing **Themes → Settings → Primary color** updates the CSS variable, which updates every template using that token.

Widgets that opt into theme tokens (rather than hardcoding their own colors) automatically follow the palette change. The NextPresso defaults do this; custom themes vary.

## Fonts

Themes ship a curated font list. Picking a font in the theme settings:

1. Sets the per-theme `display_font` / `body_font` setting.
2. Triggers the theme to emit the appropriate `<link rel="preconnect">` and `<link href="...">` tags in the head.
3. Updates the `--theme-display` / `--theme-body` CSS variables.

Most themes use Google Fonts; some support self-hosted variable fonts. The list is theme-defined; you can't pick an arbitrary font that isn't in the theme's manifest from the UI.

## Resetting a theme

**Themes → \<theme\> → "•••" → Reset all**: wipes every theme_data row for the theme and re-seeds defaults from the theme's source files. Per-theme settings are also reset.

Use the export feature first if there's anything you might want back.

## Versioning theme data

NextPress doesn't ship a built-in versioning UI for theme data, but the export file is a perfectly good versioning unit:

1. Before a big theme edit, export.
2. Commit the export to a private gist or git repo (it's small, plain JSON).
3. If you want to roll back, import the older version.

A dedicated theme-data revisions feature is in the long-term backlog.

## Tips

- **Test in preview before activating.** For an unactivated theme, use the **Preview** button on the theme card — it renders public routes with the in-progress theme while leaving the live site alone.
- **Settings save instantly.** There's no Save button; closing the tab won't lose changes. But there's also no undo — if you change a color and immediately want the old value back, you have to remember it.
- **Export before destructive changes.** "Reset all" and "Import theme data" are both destructive. Export first.
- **Keep custom templates few.** Two or three custom templates is plenty for most sites — beyond that, editorial coordination ("which template do I assign?") gets fiddly.
